title: Tapered Cedar Planter Box
summary: Angled sides, mitered corners, drainage that actually works. An hour of cuts, a lifetime of not rotting.
description:
A planter box is a bucket you built. The only thing that separates a good one from firewood is **drainage** — a box that holds water rots from the inside in two seasons, no matter what species you used.

So: real drainage holes, a false bottom with a gap, and feet that lift the whole thing off the ground. Do those three things and cedar will give you a decade.

The taper is what makes it look designed rather than nailed together. It costs you nothing but a 5° blade tilt.

tools.note: The 5° bevel rips. A miter saw can do the crosscuts but you need a tilting blade for the taper.
Mitered corners want a band clamp or tape. Bar clamps just push the miters apart.

materials.name: Cedar, 1x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Untreated. Cedar's oils are its own rot resistance.

materials.name: Exterior screws, stainless or coated, 1-5/8"
materials.note: Plain steel + cedar tannins = rust streaks down your new planter within one rainy season.

materials.name: Landscape fabric
materials.note: Lines the inside. Holds soil in, lets water out.

cutList.note: Top edges bevelled 5°. Three slats per long side.
Inside the corners. The slats screw to these — this is what actually holds the box together.
Spaced 1/2" apart. The gaps ARE the drainage.
Lifts the box off the patio so the bottom can dry out. Skip these and the bottom rots first.

steps.title: Set the blade to 5° and leave it
steps.body:
Every side slat gets its long edges ripped at 5°. That slight angle is what gives the box its taper — the sides lean out as they rise.

Cut all the bevels in one session without touching the blade angle. Consistency is what makes the corners close.

steps.title: Cut the slats
steps.body:
Six long (24"), six short (14"). Three courses of slats per side.

Cedar is soft and splintery — score your cut line or use a fine blade if the top face is tearing out.

steps.title: Screw the sides to the corner cleats
steps.body:
Stand a corner cleat vertically and screw the slats to it from the outside, one course at a time, leaving a small consistent gap between courses (or butt them tight — your call, gaps look lighter).

The cleats do the structural work. The corners are cosmetic.

steps.title: Close the box
steps.body:
Bring the four sides together around the cleats. Because of the 5° bevel, the corners should meet cleanly with the sides leaning outward.

Band-clamp or tape the corners while you drive screws. Bar clamps push mitered corners apart rather than together — don't fight it.

steps.title: Build the false bottom
steps.body:
Screw two cleats across the inside, about 2" up from the bottom edge. Lay the bottom slats on them with **1/2" gaps between every slat.**

That gap, plus the 2" air space below it, is the whole drainage strategy. Water leaves, air circulates, and the bottom of the box gets to dry out between waterings.

A solid bottom with four drilled holes will clog with soil and turn your planter into a bucket.

steps.title: Feet, liner, fill
steps.body:
Screw the four feet on. The box should sit with an air gap under it — on a patio, this is the difference between ten years and three.

Staple landscape fabric to the inside. It keeps soil from washing out through the bottom gaps while letting water through.

Fill with potting mix (not garden soil — it compacts and drowns roots in a container). Leave the cedar unfinished and let it silver.
